04:58
May 11, 2021
A Micro Lesson on cryptocurrencies (for beginners)
If you don't know a damned thing about cryptocurrencies such as Bitcoin and Dogecoin, this gives you a quick overview. That's all you'd need to get started building your talent stack with crypto knowledge. When I give advice to young people I tell them to add this skill to their talent stack right away. It pairs well with lots of what is coming, making it commercially useful in a variety of ways.
 
02:35
May 7, 2021
A Micro Lesson on the Psychology of exercising
You have to get the "thinking" about exercise right or else all the technique in the world won't help. I explain. One of the most important things you will ever learn about yourself is that your body controls your thoughts as much as your thoughts control what your body does. What this means is you can hack your happiness by manipulating what your body is doing. Don't believe me? Track your mood on any day you have exercised versus a day you did not. Also track how well you sleep that night. You'll be amazed at how different those days look, but you probably thought the non-exercise days were just the "unlucky" ones where things went wrong. That is an illusion. Exercise makes you happier. I tell you in this video how to get more of it.
 
04:38
May 5, 2021
A Micro Lesson from behind the persuasion curtain
I teach you how to recognize the massive battle of influence being played out in America.
 
03:31
May 3, 2021
A Micro Lesson on pets as an obstacle to success
I love dogs and cats, and have always had pets. But they make any kind of success in life a LOT harder. I'll tell you why. In my defense, I have only willingly obtained one pet, my cat Sarah, who lived to nineteen and was a nightmare. I never wanted another pet after that experience, although I loved her. My ex-wife got a family dog without my approval. It's one of the worst things anyone has ever done to me. I knew it would substantially degrade my quality of life for 12-17 years, and it has. Then she got two cats that couldn't get along so I ended up with one after the divorce. I have two initially-unwanted pets now, and love them, but I never would have handicapped my life this way on purpose. Don't make the same mistake.

03:53
Mar 24, 2021
A Micro Lesson in movie script construct
You might never want to write a movie. But you can quickly add this skill to your Talent Stack in a few minutes. The more talents you acquire, the more chances they will have "idea sex" and someday spawn a new idea for you, or a new way of thinking in an unrelated area. I also find it useful to understand that success can be more technique than luck or even talent. Once you learn the "formula" for a lot of different talents, success itself seems more achievable, and that's going to make you more likely to pursue it.

Ever wonder what makes a human happy? We're all different, but I'll tell you one thing that probably works for 90% of us.

Are you concerned about China eventually dominating the United States and the rest of the world? It might happen. But I predict the opposite. See why, and learn a prediction technique.

Mar 18, 2021

Have you ever noticed that some people have extraordinary touch and some don't? It's technique. I teach you how. For some of you (maybe most), this lesson will give you a relationship superpower. 02:49
Mar 9, 2021
A Micro Lesson on using persuasion to minimize the coming George Floyd riots
I'm assuming the police officer accused of killing George Floyd will not be convicted on any kind of murder count because there is reasonable doubt all over this situation. So how do we avoid the inevitable rioting that will follow it? I tell you how to create a narrative that "interferes" with the one you want to thwart.
 
05:28
Mar 6, 2021
A Micro Lesson on programming your brain via word choice
Every time you form a sentence, you program your own brain in a tiny way. If you don't learn to do it intentionally, you will be programming yourself randomly over time. I teach you how to control that process.
 
08:45
Mar 4, 2021
A Micro Lesson on The Great Filters of reality
If you want to understand reality (as well as a human can), here are the best "filters" you can acquire over your lifetime. None are difficult to learn, but it takes time to assemble this skill stack.
 
07:45
Mar 2, 2021
A Micro Lesson on how to decide a new line of business will work
I teach you how to know if a new line of business, such as a startup, is worth pursuing. My example will take you behind the curtain as I make this decision myself on a new opportunity. See how I approach it.
 
02:46
Mar 1, 2021
Micro Lesson on how to win a coin toss every time
I invented this trick, by the way. I haven't lost a coin toss since the seventies, except when someone is smart enough to not let me toss it. And then I still win half the time. Works great for negotiating with kids. It's the ultimate dad prank. You can also make people think you are psychic. It has many practical applications. By the way, kids who become obsessed with magic tricks (present!) learn early that reality is subjective, and that it can be controlled with a fairly simple user interface.
 
06:56
Feb 28, 2021
A Micro Lesson on drawing bodies, shading and perspective
This is part of my series on how to be a cartoonist. I try to make the micro lessons in this series interesting enough that you wouldn't have to care about cartooning to find value in how it is presented. And I keep them short. I'm also experimenting with the idea that micro lessons are more effective than long ones, especially as our attention spans shrink. This is my best test case.
 
09:01
Feb 27, 2021
A Micro Lesson on drawing a cartoon/comic fac
This is part of my developing series on how to be a cartoonist. Most of you don't have that ambition, but you can still add to your talent stack just by knowing how the process works. And I'll make it short and fast-paced so you can absorb it quickly. You'll be surprised how exposure to any new field or new system has spillover benefits in unexpected ways because of "idea sex." That's James Altucher's concept that new ideas are the babies of existing ideas that mated. So the more ideas/systems/worlds you experience, the more raw materials you have for future solutions to problems you don't even know you will have. My next micro lesson on this topic will include how to draw bodies and objects, including shading and perspective.
 
04:25
Feb 26, 2021
A Micro Lesson on using the 80-20 rule to build your Talent Stack
You probably already know what a Talent Stack is. I teach you an incredibly easy way to increase your talent stack without much effort at all. I introduced the idea of Talent Stacks in my 2013 book, How to Fail at Almost Everything and Still Win Big. https://www.amazon.com/How-Fail-Almost-Everything-Still-ebook/dp/B00COOFBA4/ref=zg_bs_154841011_22?_encoding=UTF8&psc=1&refRID=QABP9YXH1BN66JRG9VQM
 
14:18
Feb 23, 2021
A Micro Lesson (15 minutes) on how to become a cartoonist
Even if you don't plan to become a cartoonist, you might be interested in my tips on how to get started, created exclusively for my Locals subscribers. I don't think I am going out on a limb to say this is the best lesson ever created on how to become a cartoonist. The lesson includes a lot of non-obvious advice you'd never find anywhere else. Soon I'll add a separate lesson on how to draw comics. Today's lesson is more about the tools and techniques and how to know you have something great.
 
03:38
Feb 21, 2021
A Micro Lesson on finding your path to meaning and happiness
I turned off the paywall on this Micro Lesson so everyone can share it. One of the oddest things about free markets is that if a product is sufficiently valuable -- such as a vaccination to end a pandemic -- you can't charge for it because it would be unethical to do so. This Micro Lesson will be unusually valuable to some people. Subscribers to my Locals community have access to my growing library of micro lessons that are designed to make you happier and more effective in life. Build your talent stack the easy way.

02:21
Feb 11, 2021
A Micro Lesson on Dog Psychology
Watch me change Snickers' subjective reality in real time. The takeaway is that humans can be programmed just as easily as dogs, as evidenced by any of the popular hoaxes (Fine People Hoax, Drinking Bleach Hoax, QANON, etc.) This is a glimpse of the reality hypnotists come to understand as they learn how to program human brains. Most of what we believe is our "thinking" is actually after-the-fact rationalizations of what we were somehow programmed to do. It's a backwards filter on the world that works better than the common one that hallucinates humans to be creatures of wisdom and reason. Instead, think of humans as rationalizers for their own actions, with the real reasons for their actions a forever mystery soup of causes and effects. Watch how often that filter on life does a better job of explaining whatever is baffling you about human behavior.

Aug 24, 2020

04:19
Aug 20, 2020
A Micro Lesson on testing your product, service, or art
How do you know you built something good? The only way to know for sure is to test it. Amazingly, this is rarely done (or done right) even by the biggest companies. Yet it isn't hard to do. The fastest way to tell a product has not been tested is to try to take it out of its packaging. If you need a tool from your garage, no one ever tested that part of the experience. Or if they did, they were not able or interested in fixing it. All of this is a bad signal about the product before you even touch it. Apple never makes that mistake. Opening their products is literally a sensual experience. No tools needed. They are selling you an experience, not a product. That's a big part of why they are worth two trillion dollars. My biggest complaint is the homebuilding industry. I doubt any home built this year will have been "tested" in any way other than past sales volume and maybe some unsolicited buyer feedback. What they haven't done, obviously, is have a few families try living in a certain floor plan for a few months and recording what worked well and what annoyed. Homes are built to look good in photos and to sell. That's it. And so long as no home builder attempts to improve on that model, consumers have no power. Consider this: No architect ever has to live in the home they designed. They probably live in a home designed by someone else who didn't live in it. You don't see that in other industries. Car designers certainly drive their own designs. Chefs certainly eat their own food. There is no feedback loop for architects. They design something that looks good on paper, and if it sells well, they get more jobs. There is little incentive to improve on that. Here's more on products never tested...

08:06
Aug 8, 2020
A micro lesson on creating a diet system for yourself
An incoming call interrupted the last minute of this video, but you only miss a redundant summary that was coming. I don't tell you what to eat. I tell you how to reframe dieting as a system that you customize for yourself. In other words, I tell you how to get the mental part right. This comes from my book How to Fail at Almost Everything and Still Win Big. Since it was published, I have heard countless stories from readers who lost massive amounts of weight and kept it off using this approach. Did you know that eating pasta spikes your glycemic levels less than eating a plain white potato? If you did not know that, I have an advantage over you in losing weight. I'll pick the pasta every time and you might pick it only half the time. This is an example in which knowledge replaces calories. When I used to eat out with friends, my personal sport was looking at what they ordered and mentally correlating it with their weight. There is a nearly perfect correlation. It wasn't that they PREFERRED the less healthy foods, it was that they couldn't tell what was better than what, except for the very obvious choices. The people with the most food knowledge also had the most ideal weights for their bodies. So make sure you are learning every day about what foods are relatively better than others. Do you think you are already good at it? What's better for maintaining your weight, a carrot or lettuce, assuming the portions are designed for the same calorie count? See? Not always so obvious. You might get that one right, but these micro-choices fill our days. (The answer is lettuce.) Part of learning is experimenting. Try this: Eat a burrito for lunch and then see if it makes you sleepy. On another day, eat for lunch only protein and broccoli and see how tired you are in an hour. For me, it's not even close. Mexican food knocks me out for the day. But you might be different, so experiment on what foods make you sleepy and/or gain weight and which do not. A good example of experimenting is Subway sandwiches. Their commercials have proclaimed their food can help you lose weight. But does that even make sense when half of the sandwich is bread? So I experimented with eating as my dinner a Subway sandwich, footlong, three times a week to see what happened. I dropped weight every time, so long as the rest of my diet was unchanged. Again, I won't say this works for you. That's the whole point of experimenting. Would you eat a chunk of raw cauliflower and enjoy it? Probably not. I wouldn't. But try this: Slice it into thin chip-like sizes, then put some salt and pepper on a cutting board and rub the cauliflower chip into the mixture and eat. That small change turns it into a delicious appetizer. The color of your plate, the utensil you use, and the way the food is cut can make all the difference in how you enjoy it. So continuously experiment on ways to prepare food that is good for you but also delicious. The video says the rest.

08:47
Jul 27, 2020
Fitness Micro Lesson
These are the notes for the video below in case you want to refer to them: ----- Most important thing: Be active every day No pain no gain is for professionals. Better to leave yourself wanting to be active tomorrow. Match your energy with your exercise schedule Protein supplements are essential — smoothies, protein bars Build it into habit Reward yourself (snack, downtime) Change of scenery Stack benefits — social (tennis, club, running group) Track your mood and sleeping on exercise days Plan to be “active” 7 days a week Bring earbuds — podcasts and other Put on your workout clothes and tennis shoes if you are not in the mood to exercise and see how quickly it changes your mind by association. Learn proper exercise and try new things over time as you get bored or curious. But always focus on making it pleasant to get fit. Some things I do (or did) because I like them: Tennis (bonus social contacts) E-bike Walking The lifetime fitness system is to stay interested in your chosen fitness activities, stay active daily, turn it into a habit with reward, and learn as you go what works best for you. ----- For further reading: https://www.amazon.com/How-Fail-Almost-Everything-Still-ebook/dp/B00COOFBA4/ref=tmm_kin_swatch_0?_encoding=UTF8&qid=1595869492&sr=1-2
 
02:14
Jul 26, 2020
A Micro Lesson on how "learning" can be ten times better
Have you ever looked for a technical or household repair solution on Youtube? You can usually find what you need, but not before spending 99% of your time sorting through the wrong things that looked like the right things. That's the "last mile" problem in learning. It's great to have information about everything, but it's almost worthless if the good stuff is buried in the bad stuff and you have so spend all of your time filtering for it. It's the same with any in-person or online class. The instructor adds a lot of filler. I just want the facts. I don't have the attention span for anything else. Imagine a persuasive national standard that says all "bits" of information should be in 10-second increments and clearly labelled. It would have to be a voluntary standard, of course, but who would pick bloated content when the short form is available and more clearly labelled? YouTube can never work for this idea, at least in its current form, because it rewards creators for longer content. I guess the idea is that hooking a user on a 30-minute program keeps them watching more reliably than hoping they will surf for the same amount of time if nothing in particular interests them. So longer is better for the creator, and for YouTube, but much, much worse for the consumer. The ten-second lesson "bit" standard would work for just about any topic, I think. And obviously you would want an option to expand on the bit, or to see other people present a similar bit just to see another angle on things. And when ten seconds isn't enough, your one instruction could be in three consecutive ten-second bits. Then it's easy to review the bit you might have missed. Here's the video of me explaining the same idea, but more compelling because it has video and a story attached. You know, filler.

01:33
Jun 28, 2020
A Micro Lesson on how Hypnotists read bodies
I use my dog Snickers to demonstrate how hypnotists know their technique is working by observing tiny changes in the subject. This is the most useful thing I learned in hypnosis. You can apply it to normal social interactions, meaning you will know if the person you are engaging with is responding to you positively or negatively. It's body language, yes, but focusing on the sort of tiny changes you would not notice if you didn't train yourself to look for them. It's easy to know if someone crosses their arms. It's harder to detect their breathing pattern and subtle shifts in facial muscles. That takes practice. But it doesn't take much education. You simply have to start looking at people more closely than you normally would, looking for any change that suggests either more stress or more comfort. You'll know it when you see it, and it gets easier with practice. The "trick" is simply knowing this is a thing, and knowing you get better just by looking for it. Once you get good at detecting subtle changes, you use that knowledge to modify whatever you are doing in real time. I just taught you one of the most useful skills you will ever learn. But it won't be obvious until you get better at it.
